skill.name: swarm-advanced
skill.description: Advanced orchestration patterns for Claude Flow swarms across research, development, testing, analysis, and automation workflows.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-agent Claude Flow swarms for complex research, engineering, testing, and monitoring workflows.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and blocker patterns, but review shows most are Markdown examples for Claude Flow MCP and CLI usage. The real concern is that the skill instructs users to install and run external packages, execute project scripts, configure automation, and write backups, so it should publish with a clear execution warning rather than be blocked.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation and CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells users to install claude-flow globally and configure an MCP server through npx, then provides npx-based swarm commands. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it executes third-party tooling from the package ecosystem and should be treated as elevated risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly present and would run external package code if followed. Confidence is below very high because the behavior is disclosed and aligned with the skill purpose, with no evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Orchestrated Project Command and Automation Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates swarm tasks that run project test scripts, security scans, analysis tasks, and event-driven automation. These examples can be useful, but they may execute repository-defined scripts or automated actions with side effects in a user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands and automation triggers are clear, but they are examples rather than hidden executable code. Risk depends on the user workspace and Claude Flow runtime configuration.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Backup Example Uses Absolute Workspace Path
medium_findings.description: The memory backup example writes to an absolute workspace path. This is not malicious by itself, but it indicates filesystem write behavior that users should review before enabling backups.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backup path is explicitly shown and suggests filesystem writes. The risk is moderate because it is a documented example and does not target sensitive paths.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Claude Flow documentation and wiki pages. They create normal outbound browsing risk but do not show data exfiltration or hidden network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible reference links at the end of the documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends user data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are Descriptive Text
low_findings.description: The high blocker matches occur in ordinary prose or example labels such as description, design, architecture, analysis, and responsibilities. No evidence found of cryptographic API use, weak hashing, credential handling, or system reconnaissance code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed cited lines show documentation words and workflow descriptions, not crypto or reconnaissance primitives. This is a strong false positive from keyword matching.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections correspond to Markdown code fence delimiters around JavaScript examples. The backticks are formatting markers, not Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited syntax is Markdown fencing around examples. Some fenced examples include real commands, but the Ruby backtick execution label itself is inaccurate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party CLI Installation and npx Runtime Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses npm and npx examples for Claude Flow setup and swarm execution. Users should verify package provenance and avoid running these commands in sensitive environments without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct command examples are present. The pattern is risky because package manager commands can execute third-party code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automation and Repository Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples coordinate npm test scripts and event-driven actions. This can run repository-controlled scripts and should require user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution paths are visible examples, not concealed behavior. Risk depends on the scripts and events configured by the user.

content.value_statement: Complex research and engineering work often needs several specialized agents working in parallel. This skill provides Claude Flow patterns for planning, coordinating, monitoring, and improving multi-agent workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains mesh, hierarchical, star, and ring swarm topologies for different workflow types.
Shows how to spawn specialized research, development, testing, analysis, and documentation agents.
Provides phased patterns for research collection, architecture work, implementation, testing, and reporting.
Demonstrates Claude Flow MCP calls for memory, monitoring, quality checks, workflows, and metrics.
Includes CLI fallback examples for users who prefer command-line swarm execution.
Covers advanced operations such as recovery, backups, learning patterns, scaling, and health checks.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow or compatible MCP tooling to run the examples.
Many examples are templates and need adaptation before production use.
CLI and automation examples can execute external tools or project scripts.
Does not provide a complete application or standalone swarm runtime.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Research Synthesis
use_cases.description: Plan a research swarm that gathers sources, validates findings, analyzes patterns, and produces a structured report.

use_cases.title: Run Multi-Agent Development
use_cases.description: Organize architecture, frontend, backend, database, testing, documentation, and deployment work across specialized agents.

use_cases.title: Improve Testing and Analysis
use_cases.description: Use star or mesh topologies to coordinate test suites, performance checks, security scans, and quality reports.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Swarm Topology
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced skill to recommend a topology for my workflow. My goal is [goal], my constraints are [constraints], and I can use [number] agents.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Research Swarm Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced research pattern to design a swarm for [topic]. Include agent roles, phases, memory usage, validation steps, and final deliverables.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Development and Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced development and testing patterns for [project]. Produce a phased plan with agents, dependencies, quality gates, test execution, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Resilient Automation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced techniques to design a fault-tolerant automation workflow for [system]. Include recovery, memory snapshots, metrics, scaling, and approval points before commands run.

output_examples.input: Plan a swarm for researching AI policy trends.
output_examples.output: A mesh research plan with web research, academic research, data analysis, source validation, synthesis, memory storage, and report generation phases.
A hierarchical development workflow with architecture first, parallel backend and frontend work, database design, test coordination, review, documentation, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: Set up a testing swarm for a web application.
output_examples.output: A star topology plan with unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security testing agents coordinated through monitored execution and quality assessment.

best_practices: Match topology to workflow structure before spawning agents.
Require approval before running CLI, package manager, automation, or repository script examples.
Use memory namespaces, checkpoints, and monitoring for long-running swarm workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run npx or npm examples in a sensitive workspace without reviewing the package and command.
Do not assign overlapping responsibilities to many agents without a coordination plan.
Do not enable event-driven automation before defining rollback, logging, and human approval steps.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps plan and coordinate advanced Claude Flow swarm workflows for research, development, testing, analysis, and automation.

faq.question: Does it require Claude Flow?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples assume Claude Flow MCP tools or the Claude Flow CLI are available.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to run every example directly?
faq.answer: No. Review commands, package installation, automation triggers, and file backup paths before execution.

faq.question: Which topology should I start with?
faq.answer: Use mesh for research, hierarchical for structured development, star for testing, and ring for staged pipelines.

faq.question: Does it include complete production code?
faq.answer: No. It provides orchestration patterns and examples that must be adapted to your tools and repository.
